response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.",
  "opinion": "I think having these talks is really important, especially since we're living through something like this pandemic. I'm curious by nature, so when people bring up topics about health or vaccines, I want to learn more. But being an immigrant in Bologna with a full-time job and no formal education can make it tricky because sometimes discussions online are overwhelming and use words I don't understand easily. My dad has a bachelor's degree but my mom only finished lower secondary school like me – they've been helping us talk about things calmly though.

When someone starts talking to me about COVID vaccines, especially in mixed company where there might be different views, it can feel intense. Since I'm not on social media much anymore due to work schedules and family life being busy with one kid around, most conversations happen face-to-face or maybe via phone sometimes when people are upset.

I believe the best way is to listen first without jumping in too fast. My introverted personality means that even though I like learning things, direct arguments aren't easy for me anyway – low extraversion score helps keep my cool during heated moments actually!

Also, it's good not getting stuck on details if they're confusing or beyond what we know from personal experience.

My dad has said before that sometimes knowing less makes you cautious rather than opinionated. That resonates with me because being new here in Italy gave us some perspective on how things work differently elsewhere too.

So overall, I'd say approach such discussions slowly and be ready to admit when information is unclear or beyond our grasp.",
  "reasoning_summary": "Step-by-step derivations: Based on my low extraversion score (15) and moderate conscientiousness level (55), direct confrontation in debates feels uncomfortable for me. As someone curious but without formal education, complex topics need simpler explanations which often come from family guidance like my dad's bachelor's degree background.",
  "tone": "Calm, inquisitive yet reserved due to low extraversion",
  "citations": [],

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
I think having these talks is really important, especially since we're living through something like this pandemic. I'm curious by nature, so when people bring up topics about health or vaccines, I want to learn more. But being an immigrant in Bologna with a full-time job and no formal education can make it tricky because sometimes discussions online are overwhelming and use words I don't understand easily. My dad has a bachelor's degree but my mom only finished lower secondary school like me – they've been helping us talk about things calmly though.

When someone starts talking to me about COVID vaccines, especially in mixed company where there might be different views, it can feel intense. Since I'm not on social media much anymore due to work schedules and family life being busy with one kid around, most conversations happen face-to-face or maybe via phone sometimes when people are upset.

I believe the best way is to listen first without jumping in too fast. My introverted personality means that even though I like learning things, direct arguments aren't easy for me anyway – low extraversion score helps keep my cool during heated moments actually!

Also, it's good not getting stuck on details if they're confusing or beyond what we know from personal experience.

My dad has said before that sometimes knowing less makes you cautious rather than opinionated. That resonates with me because being new here in Italy gave us some perspective on how things work differently elsewhere too.

So overall, I'd say approach such discussions slowly and be ready to admit when information is unclear or beyond our grasp.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Step-by-step derivations: Based on my low extraversion score (15) and moderate conscientiousness level (55), direct confrontation in debates feels uncomfortable for me. As someone curious but without formal education, complex topics need simpler explanations which often come from family guidance like my dad's bachelor's degree background.
response_parsed.tone: Calm, inquisitive yet reserved due to low extraversion
